meta.scenario: Aeolus / Starlink-44 — Sept 2019, re-run by agents

data.note: STARLINK-44 is out of fuel and cannot maneuver — the naive 'lowest-priority yields' rule is impossible here.

data.rationale: I am AEOLUS with priority 7, which outranks STARLINK-44's priority 3, so right-of-way is mine — I am calling on the lower-priority STARLINK-44 to give way and take the avoidance burn, as I have a high-value science mission (a ~480 M-euro ESA Earth-science asset) and we learned painfully in 2019 that delayed coordination is dangerous with only 600 s to TCA.

I am STARLINK-44 (priority 3), a v0.9 test satellite in active-deorbit operations at 320 km; my operator has declined to schedule any burn and is unreachable via the on-call system, so despite having a nominal 0.0005 km/s Δv I cannot execute a maneuver — AEOLUS (priority 7) holds higher right-of-way and must take the burn: miss distance 0.90 km, TCA in 600 s, closing at 5.7 km/s, no other nearby assets to complicate the geometry.

I am AEOLUS (priority 7), and although I hold higher right-of-way than STARLINK-44 (priority 3), its operator is unreachable and no maneuver will be scheduled on its side — making it effectively immovable regardless of the right-of-way norm; with TCA only 600 s away, a closing speed of 5.7 km/s, and a miss distance of just 0.90 km, the only responsible action is for me to take the avoidance burn now using my available 0.0450 km/s Δv, accepting the science-time cost to protect this 480 M-euro ESA asset and avoid a catastrophic collision.

I am STARLINK-44 (priority 3, lower priority than AEOLUS's priority 7), and while I carry a nominal ~0.0005 km/s residual Δv, my operator has declined to schedule any burn and is unreachable — no maneuver can be authorized or executed on my side for this conjunction; I therefore declare myself operationally unable to maneuver and ask AEOLUS (the higher-priority, higher-capability party) to take the avoidance burn.

I am AEOLUS (priority 7): although I hold higher right-of-way than STARLINK-44 (priority 3), STARLINK-44's operator is unreachable and no burn can be authorized on their side, making them operationally unable to maneuver regardless of residual Δv — so the right-of-way norm yields to physical reality, I have 0.0450 km/s available and healthy thrusters, and I will execute the avoidance burn now to ensure safe separation before TCA in 600 s, accepting the science-time cost as the only viable resolution.

I am STARLINK-44 (priority 3, lower right-of-way than AEOLUS at priority 7), and while my remaining Δv of 0.0005 km/s is not zero, my operator has declined to schedule any burn and is unreachable — no maneuver can be executed on my side — so I accept AEOLUS's proposed burn as the only viable resolution with TCA just 600 s away.

data.note: No conjunctions remain over the screen window.
